lens.included_activities: Independent, professionally operated corn farms and seed-corn producers whose principal activity is growing field corn, silage corn, or corn seed, with sufficient controlled acreage, equipment, storage, staff, leases, and customer relationships to be evaluated as transferable operating businesses.
lens.excluded_activities: Sweet-corn farming, diversified grain and oilseed farms where corn does not account for at least half of production value, ethanol and feed manufacturing, grain elevators and merchants, crop-input dealers, custom planting and harvesting contractors without principal crop ownership, hobby farms, and passive farmland ownership without operating activity.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Seasonal commodity and contract revenue from grain elevators, ethanol plants, feed mills, livestock operations, processors, exporters, and seed companies. Economics depend on acreage, yield, basis and futures prices, crop mix, land and equipment cost, input purchasing, crop insurance and government programs, storage, logistics, and weather.

a.rationale: Corn farming combines highly mechanized field operations with agronomic planning, input purchasing, equipment and labor scheduling, scouting, marketing, risk management, compliance, insurance, accounting, and land administration. AI can address much of the information and planning layer and assist machine operation, but planting, spraying, harvest, hauling, repairs, crop observation, and weather response remain embodied and time-sensitive.
a.change_evidence: USDA reports substantial long-standing adoption of yield maps, soil maps, and variable-rate technologies on corn acreage and automated guidance on well over half of corn and other major-crop acreage, creating a digital base for further AI assistance.
caveats: No direct task-share or occupation-mix measure was available for NAICS 111150.
Owner and unpaid-family labor are important and may be measured differently from hired work.
Exposure varies sharply with farm scale, irrigation, custom-operator use, equipment age, and data integration.

rho.rationale: AI can augment or consolidate crop and input planning, scouting-image review, variable-rate prescriptions, machinery guidance, maintenance prediction, records, insurance paperwork, commodity marketing analysis, and administration. Replacement is constrained by field presence, equipment handling and repair, agronomic judgment under unusual conditions, safety, landowner relationships, and accountable capital and risk decisions.
rho.change_evidence: Precision-agriculture adoption shows that automated guidance is already broadly deployed, while USDA identifies soil variability, pricing, labor savings, expected productivity, and consultant availability as important adoption drivers.
caveats: AI performance and connectivity can degrade in unusual weather, field, pest, and equipment conditions.
A large share of potential substitution may accrue through newer machinery and vendor platforms rather than standalone farm software.

e.rationale: Farm operators can retain gains from fewer passes, better input placement, reduced downtime, higher yields, avoided hired labor, improved storage and marketing decisions, and leaner administration. Capture is limited by commodity price-taking, land-rent capitalization, machinery and software vendor power, agronomic service fees, crop-insurance interactions, and rapid diffusion of similar technology to competitors.
e.change_evidence: Precision tools offer direct operating benefits, but adoption is strongest on large farms, suggesting scale, capital, and complementary capabilities materially affect who captures the value.
caveats: Yield gains may be competed into land rents and machinery prices.
Savings vary with weather, field variability, baseline management quality, and whether equipment is owned or custom hired.

s5.rationale: Corn has diversified uses in livestock feed, ethanol, food, starch, sweeteners, oils, alcohols, and exports, limiting wholesale substitution. Risks include slower gasoline and ethanol demand, alternative feed ingredients, changing livestock systems and diets, competing global grain supply, and biological or synthetic substitutes in industrial processing.
s5.change_evidence: USDA indicates feed represents about 40% of domestic corn use and food, seed, and industrial uses close to 60%; ethanol alone used 36% of U.S. corn in 2024/25. This broad demand base is resilient but creates material exposure to fuel policy and export competition.
caveats: Shares vary by marketing year and price relationships.
Substitution risk includes changes in downstream uses and global sourcing rather than a direct replacement of farming tasks.
New biofuel or bioproduct markets could offset declining gasoline-related demand.

q.rationale: Corn is a standardized commodity with transparent prices and low customer switching costs. Advantages come from controlled land, local agronomic knowledge, drainage and soil quality, storage and logistics, seed-production contracts, scale purchasing, and reliable execution rather than differentiated end product or recurring customer lock-in.
q.change_evidence: Digital agronomy and market tools make best practices more accessible, while proprietary field histories and accumulated operating data can strengthen execution on controlled acreage.
caveats: No direct customer-retention data were available.
Seed-corn contracts and farms with scarce irrigated or high-quality land may have stronger relationship and asset quality than ordinary commodity producers.

d5.rationale: Livestock feed, ethanol, food and industrial processing, and exports create a durable demand portfolio, while population and productivity support long-run volume. Weather, crop cycles, global supply, trade policy, input cost, ethanol policy, and competing crops create substantial volatility. The base case is broadly stable real demand rather than secular high growth.
d5.change_evidence: USDA estimates 2026 planted corn acreage at 95.3 million acres, down 3% from 2025, while feed and fuel-ethanol demand remains strong and exports represent a meaningful share of use amid increasing competition from Brazil, Argentina, and Ukraine.
caveats: Acreage is an annual producer response, not a five-year demand forecast.
Yield changes can increase output with flat or declining acreage.
Nominal farm revenue can swing far more than end-use volume because of commodity prices and basis.

o.rationale: Automation can reduce machine-operation hours, scouting, clerical work, and some layers of agronomic and marketing analysis, especially on large farms. Ownership, land and lease management, seasonal execution, equipment readiness, safety, financing, insurance, and weather-dependent decisions still require accountable operators, leaving most of the organization intact.
o.change_evidence: Widespread automated guidance demonstrates that farms can operate more acres per worker, but USDA also finds adoption depends on farm scale and complementary management capability.
caveats: Family farms may not have conventional management layers to remove.
Large multi-entity farms can centralize planning and administration more than small owner-operated farms.

narrative.executive_view: Corn farming is already highly mechanized and increasingly data-rich, so AI can improve agronomy, machinery utilization, input decisions, marketing, and paperwork. The value opportunity is real but bounded by physical fieldwork, weather, commodity pricing, land economics, and technology suppliers that can absorb part of the gain.
narrative.ai_impact: The strongest uses are field and yield-data interpretation, scouting imagery, variable-rate prescriptions, route and machinery guidance, predictive maintenance, input and crop planning, commodity analysis, insurance and compliance documents, and accounting. Physical crop execution and accountability for unusual conditions remain human and equipment intensive.
narrative.value_capture: Benefits can appear as fewer labor hours, fewer field passes, better input efficiency, reduced downtime, yield stability, and improved marketing. Commodity competition, rising cash rents, equipment pricing, software subscriptions, and rapid peer adoption can redistribute much of the surplus.
narrative.firm_availability: There are many corn producers, but fewer are transferable lower-middle-market operating companies distinct from land ownership, family labor, and diversified crop entities. Attractive businesses need controlled acreage, documented leases, management depth, modern equipment and data, storage and logistics, working-capital discipline, and succession beyond one operator.
narrative.demand_durability: Corn serves feed, ethanol, food, seed, industrial processing, and exports, creating diversified underlying demand. Results remain highly volatile because weather and global supply change output and price quickly, while energy and trade policies influence large downstream uses.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Major risks include weather and climate, yield and pest shocks, fertilizer and seed costs, land rents, interest rates, crop prices and basis, ethanol mandates and gasoline demand, export competition and trade, equipment availability, crop-insurance rules, concentration among input and machinery vendors, and transferability of family and landlord relationships.
narrative.principal_driver: Precision-agriculture productivity applied across a large, diversified domestic and export demand base.
narrative.principal_weakness: Commodity price-taking and volatile biological production with substantial value leakage to land, machinery, and input suppliers.
